extra.reasoning:
The property G(opted_out implies not sent) requires that no reachable state has opted_out=true and sent=true simultaneously. Transition t5 directly defeats this: on send_attempt, when opted_out=true AND ticks>=10, it sets sent:=true. So if t5 is ever enabled and applied, the resulting state has opted_out=true and sent=true, violating the property.

Reachability of t5's guard: Start at opted_out=false, sent=false, ticks=0. Fire optout (t1): opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=0. Fire tick ten times (t4): each tick increments ticks (capped at 10) and sets sent=false; after 10 ticks the state is opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=10. Now fire send_attempt: the guard of t5 (opted_out and ticks>=10) holds, so t5 applies sent:=true, yielding opted_out=true, sent=true, ticks=10 — a violating state.

Note that under send_attempt with opted_out=true and ticks>=10 both t3 (sets sent:=false) and t5 (sets sent:=true) are enabled, a genuine conflict. But t5 is an explicitly defined enabled transition, so the system can take it, producing the bad state. There is no stated priority preventing t5, and any interpretation that allows t5 to fire reaches the violation. The witnessing execution is 12 events long, within the 14-event bound. Hence the property is violated.
